item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image is by the Mary Evans Picture Library, and the copyright is retained by them. As they have not released the image under Creative Commons nor given us permission to use this image, it has since been taken down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ahh, but that's the kicker, people simply cannot resist. It's more than just habit, their hormonal balance is disturbed and they'll constantly be under that hunger drive for food-seeking behaviour. Just look at how difficult it is for obese people to try weight control via diet & exercise, the only true solution/cure. Thus we keep making pills for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a pretty good SCP in my opinion. I don't have much experience with them, aside from the reading of the ones before this, but this seems like a very well-done one.
Also, simple, but possible(?) solution.
What if you were to just…
Refuse to eat?
Could that kill it off, or would it outlast the person who has it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the details put into this , besides SCP-2680 this is an amazing virus SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy cow! I wrote this yeeeeears ago and forgot all about. I'm so happy so many people liked it!

item.title: Spontaneous fire
item.content:encoded: Spontainius combustion a.k.a CATCHING ON FLIPPIN FIRE… this one is scary

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the rare uncontained SCPs that I've upvoted. About the only ones that I'll let get by me uncontained are microbiological.

+1

I meant to upvote this a long time ago but I didn't have an account when I first read it.
Anyway, congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It IS "just" a deadly disease. Society's reactions are another story. It's one thing when people get sick and die. We're used to that progression. However, "My spouse suddenly went from not feeling well to eating everything in the house, garden and McDonald's down the street before she burst in to flames" is a little more panic-inducing. Doubly so when it gets out that this is incurable. To protect the world from itself, that's why this is an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well if the first set of Symptoms come the second week then Corentine should be set to two weeks at least? And very good story, but it makes me think that this is just a deadly disease like necrosis.

But overall approved!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I was little, I saw a documentary on spontaneous human combustion. I had to sleep with lights on in my room.

Now thank you for rekindling1 my old fear.

Footnotes
1. No pun intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not magic. It's too weird to let anyone know about, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the first SCP I read after a month-long break. Gave me the chills. +1
Also: I've found that Cryokina's SCP Foundation Theme Song Playlist goes quite well with the articles here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Problem: There is a potential error in this SCP's terminology.

Explanation: You mention, in the 'Stage 3' section that 'infected subjects display extreme hyperphagia'. The term I believe you intended to use is 'Polyphagia'. Both are conditions characterised by excessive hunger, however Hyperphagia is a subset of Polyphagia believed to occur due to damage or lesion on the Hyperthalmus, whereas Polyphagia is a more generalised term usually caused by metabolic pathobiologies. Unless the virus does indeed cause brain damage, in which case I recant this statement.

Additional: I enjoyed this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would adapt and evolve into Charizard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They already did much worse and It's still alive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nix that sig.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if you exposed it to SCP-682?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't necessarily need to be paranormal. Surgeon Crabs aren't paranormal. Just dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A virus that makes you burst into flame isn't anomalous?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You wrote a nice SCP, but like with most of the ones about diseases and contagions, neglected to include anything paranormal or 'anamolous' in it. Why would the Foundation have anything to do with controlling a conventional disease? It doesn't fit in with the mandate I don't think, so I wish there was a paranormal element here. Still, not voting down because it is well-written and thought out, except for that omission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edits made. Feel free to undo if you feel it's too inaccessible this way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this SCP; it lets me explain why I'm fat to people…

A minor note: Right now, the entry says "adipose cells" and "white blood cells." For consistency, it should probably either use the colloquial in both cases, "fat cells" and "white blood cells," or the clinical, "adipocytes" and "leukocytes."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my most favorite SCPs. It reminds me of when my father would always tell me I could spontaneously combust at any moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One nitpick: "SCP-081 is estimated to have existed since the 9██ and thought to have originated in █████████████." The first blackout, presumably of a date, seems either pointless or just misstructured. Is it supposed to be something like "since the █th Century"?

Otherwise… it's so crazy it just might work! Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't be surprised if this was actually true.

Have a +.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that aspect of biological SCP's can be a strength, personally, because you can do some pretty outlandish things (this article is an example) and they still seem strangely possible. It's a nice shift from "omg an invincible unstoppable killing machine that makes you go crazy and requires the sacrifice of an orphan every day to keep it contained". I can almost imagine it to be true, and therefore find it more creepy. Again, just my personal tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, sure, that's why those conditions still appear int he gene pool. If they were entirely negative, they would have died out. Instead, you get areas where sometimes it's a detriment and other times a boon. That's often the trouble with making a biological SCP. There's really more weirdness out then than we're comfortable with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely like the touch of (at least some types of) diabetes providing an immunity, since similar benefits from otherwise deleterious conditions have been noted (sickle-cell preventing malaria, for example).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering matters, I'd think type II (or adult onset) diabetes would be more likely to have it occur, due to the symptoms. Unless you're proposing the fat cells tolerance of insulin is a contributing factor for the immunity?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'Diabetes' seems too general, since it includes insipidus which has nothing to do with metabolism. Should say diabetes mellitus, or type I diabetes if that's what you mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bonus points for having the outline of a plausible mechanism for it. Maybe massive simultaneous catalysis of ATP could be what actually sparks the fire? Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw the classic spontaneous combustion photo and upvoted immediately. In hindsight, it amazes me that this hasn't been done before.
